thai: ไก่ทอดกรอบส่วนก้น

food_subgroup: Fried poultry — chicken tail / rump cuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried chicken tail or rump. FOODON_03301116 (fried chicken food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried chicken meat generically; no specific code exists for chicken tail/parson's nose cuts. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried chicken tail or equivalent cut. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of crispy fried chicken tail consists of approximately 6 to 8 pieces weighing around 150 g, as commonly served as a snack or appetiser portion in Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, back, meat and skin, cooked, fried, flour (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 05063)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken tail (parson's nose) is a high-fat cut anatomically adjacent to the back/rump. No dedicated USDA or HPB entry exists for chicken tail specifically. USDA SR Legacy entry for chicken back, meat and skin, fried in flour (NDB 05063) is used as the closest defensible analogue, as both cuts share high skin-to-meat ratio and similar fat content. Values are adapted with a modest upward adjustment to fat and energy to reflect the higher fat content of the tail cut relative to the back. Protein is slightly lower in the tail due to higher fat proportion. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken tail. GI is estimated at 45 based on the analogous entry for battered/flour-coated fried chicken (GI ~45, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The carbohydrate contribution is primarily from the flour batter coating. GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 6.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 4.4. Classified as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce, seasoning, and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of crispy fried chicken tail due to its high sodium content (~720 mg per 150 g serving), which represents approximately 31% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; portion control and avoidance of additional dipping sauces is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from flour batter coating combined with high fat content affecting postprandial insulin response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume crispy fried chicken tail in small portions occasionally, as the low GI (~45) limits rapid glucose spikes, but the high total fat content (22 g per 100 g) and caloric density (320 kcal per 100 g) may contribute to weight gain and impaired insulin sensitivity if consumed regularly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium content from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of crispy fried chicken tail, as its high saturated fat (~6.2 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g), and sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) content collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; grilled or steamed chicken alternatives are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Chicken Tail (Thai: ไก่ทอดกรอบส่วนก้น, romanized: Kai Thot Krob Suan Kon) is a Thai-style deep-fried snack made from the tail or parson's nose cut of the chicken — the fatty, skin-rich rump portion located at the posterior end of the bird. This cut is prized in Thai street food and casual dining for its exceptionally crispy skin, rich flavour, and succulent, gelatinous texture due to its high fat and collagen content. The chicken tails are typically marinated in a mixture of garlic, coriander root, fish sauce, white pepper, and sometimes oyster sauce, then coated in seasoned wheat flour or tempura batter before being deep-fried in hot vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The result is a bite-sized piece with a shatteringly crispy exterior and a juicy, fatty interior. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms as a quick-bite appetiser or snack. Nutritionally, crispy fried chicken tail is calorie-dense (~320 kcal per 100 g) with high fat content (~22 g per 100 g), moderate protein (~22.5 g per 100 g), and relatively low carbohydrate (~6.5 g per 100 g) from the batter coating. Its sodium content is elevated (~480 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce and seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it less likely to cause rapid blood glucose spikes, though its high fat and caloric density warrant moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or Type 2 Diabetes. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or fish-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy chicken tail?
What is parson's nose fried chicken?
Is chicken tail high in fat?
Can diabetics eat fried chicken tail?
Is crispy chicken tail high in sodium?

chicken tail: ส่วนก้นไก่

confidence_reason: No dedicated nutrient entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for chicken tail as a distinct cut. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (chicken back, meat and skin, fried in flour, NDB 05063) with adjustments reflecting the higher fat proportion of the tail cut. GI is estimated from analogous battered fried chicken data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact source match for this specific cut.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05063 (chicken back, meat and skin, fried, flour) as the closest available analogue for chicken tail. Fat and energy values have been adjusted upward to reflect the higher skin-to-meat and fat-to-protein ratio of the tail cut. This is an estimated adaptation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. 4. GI is estimated at 45 based on analogous battered fried chicken data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for chicken tail exists in approved sources. 5. Halal status is marked false as this dish contains fish sauce (nam pla), which may not be certified halal depending on the restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific establishment. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu name 'Crispy Chicken Backside Delight' to the canonical English descriptor 'Crispy Fried Chicken Tail'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for fried chicken meat; this is a naturally zero value for this food type with no plant-derived vitamin C sources in the core preparation.
